pressure_handling: Balancing a full academic load, an active IT internship, and part-time work has required real prioritization discipline. When systems or workflows break under pressure, I focus first on understanding the architecture of the problem before acting — which usually produces faster and more durable fixes than jumping to solutions.
problem_solving_example: At the FSU IT internship, I identified a recurring inefficiency in how support tickets were being routed and resolved. Rather than patching individual cases, I mapped the full workflow, found the structural bottleneck, and implemented a targeted automation that eliminated the repetitive step. The approach — system-level diagnosis before solution design — is the same one I apply to any consulting or architecture problem.
ai_implementation_example: Built this interactive resume using a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline — embedding structured personal data, retrieving semantically relevant context per query, and feeding it to an LLM. The goal wasn't to impress with AI buzzwords, but to solve a real problem: how do you make a resume interactive and accurate without hallucination? Designing the architecture, choosing the right models, and tuning the retrieval threshold required understanding the system deeply, not just calling an API.
teamwork_example: At FSU ITS, I regularly translate technical findings to non-technical staff and administrators. The challenge is communicating architecture decisions — why one approach is more maintainable, why automation here saves time there — in language that maps to their actual concerns rather than technical vocabulary.
conflict_style: Prefer structured analysis over emotional reaction. When there's disagreement on an approach, I find it more productive to make the tradeoffs explicit — what does each option cost, what does it gain — so the decision is based on the system's actual constraints rather than preference.
